published_date: 2026-05-19
video_id: 9RxCzUtfRKs
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9RxCzUtfRKs
title: The Macro Minute: Are investors too all in on AI?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale,
presenting our macro minute for Tuesday,
May 19th, 2026. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
Lead-Off Morning Note, so let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
are investors too all-in on AI? Our
answer is perhaps. Bank of America's
Global Fund Manager Survey showed equity
exposure surged to a net 50% overweight
from 13% in the largest monthly increase
on record, marking the most bullish
positioning since the everything bubble
peaked in January of 2022. Long
semiconductors ranked as the most
crowded trade at 73% of respondents. The
survey provides evidence that our
paradigm CEA aka crowded hot theme is
fully priced into the stock market and
credit markets, with only 4% of
investors now expecting a hard landing,
and expectations for double-digit EPS
growth posting a record jump. Meanwhile,
just 16% foresee the Fed rate hikes uh
in 2026, alongside 66% expecting the
Hormuz bottleneck to end within months.
All told, we continue to highlight the
risk that the Fed is gearing up to take
steps to counter a US economy that is
being run too hot at the current
juncture. If the Fed fails to take away
the punch bowl, the highest probability
outcome is that the stock market
bubbles, in line with our views from
last fall. Refer to our September 24th
Lead-Off Morning Note and December 13th
Around the Horn for more details. And
I'll tell you right now, the difference
between an institutional investor who
makes millions of dollars per annum to
do this versus uh novice retail investor
sitting at home struggling to stay on
the right side of market risk is the
difference between having to make that
choice now or listening to the wisdom of
the crowd when the market decides it has
to make the choice. You don't have to
decide today whether the market will
bubble or whether the market is about to
pull back in a meaningful manner. You
don't have to make that decision. It's
far better, at least statistically
speaking,
to be the second investor in a confirmed
trade rather than the first investor in
a trade that hasn't been yet confirmed
by other market participants. That's
where type two errors live. You don't
want type two errors. Those are the more
hazardous
errors from
from the perspective of tracking error,
from the perspective of maximum
drawdowns. You want to limit the errors
that you make in your portfolio to
small, manageable type one errors that
come as a result of trying to listen to
the wisdom of the crowd and staying on
the right side of market risk and
ultimately the crowd having to change
its mind on a time horizon that is
shorter than you initially expected.
That's fine. Those are okay. You don't
want to have the market change its mind
in a way that's counter to what you
think by your lonesome because that's
when a bubble can run away from you if
you're not long it and that's when a
bear market can run away from you if
you're still long. So, as always, we'll
wrap up with a question from our
community. This one's titled source of
funds. Are gold and silver going through
the source of funds rotation that we
expected from Max 7? I'm a little
surprised by their continued weakness in
light of sovereign debt risk, which is
as far as I can see has not abated at
all. Perhaps countries affected by oil
instability are being forced to source
liquidity. So, the first thing I'll say
is
the the our views on gold in terms of
being the diversifier out of the
geopolitically driven supply and demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market is
the fact that it is a bond diversifier.
What's happening to gold is a several
things starting back in late January is
the nomination of Kevin Warsh for Fed
chair really called into question the
Federal Reserve's outlook, the
structural outlook for the Federal
Reserve's reaction function particularly
as it relates to the balance sheet and
long-term the long-term outlook for
monetary debasement here in the US
economy. So, that was obviously a big
issue. And then just an equally large if
not larger issue is the fact that
central banks around the world,
particularly those that are most
impacted by the closure of the Strait of
Hormuz, and their inability to source,
you know, the the FX reserves that they
need to to prevent balance of payments
issues in their economies, they've taken
to the market to sell gold to raise
funds to protect their domestic
industry. So, you've had two
issues that have been weighing on the
price of gold from the sector of the
monetary policy cycle, and also from the
sector of the liquidity cycle. And and
and those are things that could actually
get worse in the coming months should we
see a Fed catch-up trade, and or should
the Strait of Hormuz remain closed
longer than the immediate investor
thinks it's likely to remain closed. So,
we're wrapping up there. Terry Sandquist
here presenting our Macro Minute for
Tuesday, May 19th, 2026. Best of luck
out there today. We'll catch back here
tomorrow. Cheers.
